title: Azure Data Engineer Knowledge Test
description: Test your understanding of Azure Data Engineer with 25 questions.

questions.question: Design a scalable streaming and batch data pipeline on Azure that ingests real-time app events and batch enrichment data into a lakehouse, ensuring data lineage, schema drift handling, and per-tenant isolation. Specify components, data models, and failure modes?

questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked
Rationale for real-world complexity: hybrid streaming + batch, governance, schema drift, and multi-tenant isolation.

## Key Concepts
- Delta Lake schema evolution and constraints
- Data lineage with Purview
- Lakehouse across ADLS Gen2, Databricks, Synapse
- Streaming vs batch, watermarking, late data handling
- RBAC per tenant and cost optimization

## Code Example
```sql
MERGE INTO silver.events t
USING bronze.events s
ON t.event_id = s.event_id
WHEN MATCHED THEN UPDATE SET t.payload = s.payload, t.updated_at = current_timestamp()
WHEN NOT MATCHED THEN INSERT (event_id, payload, updated_at) VALUES (s.event_id, s.payload, current_timestamp())
```

## Follow-up Questions
- How would you test schema drift and automated backfills?
- How would you enforce data masking for sensitive fields in lakehouse?

questions.question: Design an Azure data lakehouse workflow to ingest daily partner CSVs into ADLS Gen2, where each partner uses differing column orders and types. Build a two-layer pipeline (Bronze: raw, Silver: normalized) with Data Factory and Databricks Delta, enabling automatic schema inference, drift handling, and incremental loads to a 4-table star schema in Synapse. Include Purview lineage and data quality gates that fail on drift threshold breaches?

options.text: Use Data Factory to enumerate and copy to landing, a Mapping Data Flow to normalize to a shared schema, and a Synapse MERGE-based upsert into dimension and fact tables. Save per-file schema manifests

Bronze: land per-partner CSVs in ADLS Gen2. Silver: Databricks notebooks infer and normalize schema per file (handle column reordering, type casting, defaults) to a canonical schema. Gold: upsert into

Proposed architecture: Ingest via Event Hubs, process in Azure Databricks with Delta Lake on ADLS Gen2, upsert into a Synapse Delta table using MERGE with mergeSchema enabled. Use Structured Streaming
Use Azure Data Factory: Copy to land raw CSVs as Parquet in ADLS Gen2, Data Flow with allowSchemaDrift to map evolving columns to a fixed star-schema in Azure Synapse, sink into a dedicated SQL pool.

questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked

Tests ability to design a resilient, scalable lakehouse that handles heterogenous partner schemas, with governance and quality gates.

## Key Concepts

- Bronze/Silver/Gold lakehouse pattern
- Schema drift detection and normalization
- Databricks Delta Lake on ADLS Gen2
- Incremental upserts into a star schema in Synapse
- Data governance and lineage with Purview
- Automated remediation and alerting on drift

## Code Example

```python
# Example: align per-file schema to a canonical order
from pyspark.sql.functions import col
path = '/mnt/bronzew/partnerA/*.csv'
df = spark.read.options(header=True, inferSchema=True).csv(path)
canonical = ['partner_id','event_time','metric','value']
df2 = df.select(*canonical)
df2.write.format('delta').mode('append').save('/mnt/silver/partnerA/')
```

## Follow-up Questions

- How would you tune drift detection thresholds and alerting?
- How would you handle new partners introducing entirely new fields without breaking the pipeline?

questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked
Tests ability to design enterprise-grade streaming pipelines with dynamic schema handling, dimensional modeling requirements, comprehensive lineage tracking, and strategic platform selection between major Spark offerings.

## Key Concepts
- Structured Streaming integration with Azure Event Hubs
- Delta Lake schema evolution and automated drift handling
- MERGE-based SCD Type 2 dimensional implementations
- Azure Purview lineage capture and metadata management
- Auto-scaling and cost optimization strategies for Spark platforms
- Strategic partitioning and watermarking configuration
- Platform comparison: Synapse Spark vs Databricks trade-offs

## Code Example
```javascript
// Pseudo-code: Databricks Delta Lake SCD2 implementation
const customerDimension = DeltaTable.forPath(spark, 'abfss://container@account.dfs.core.windows.net/delta/customers')
const streamingDF = spark.readStream.format('eventhubs')
  .option('eventhubs.connectionString', connectionString)
  .load()
  .select(from_json(col('body').cast('string'), schema).alias('data'))
  .select('data.*')

streamingDF.writeStream.format('delta')
  .foreachBatch((batchDF, batchId) => {
    customerDimension.as('target')
      .merge(batchDF.as('source'), 'target.customer_id = source.customer_id')
      .whenMatched().updateAll()
      .whenNotMatched().insertAll()
      .execute()
  })
  .option('checkpointLocation', '/checkpoints/customers')
  .start()
```

## Strategic Considerations
Choose Databricks over Synapse for superior Delta Lake integration, advanced auto-scaling capabilities, and optimized streaming performance. Configure partitioning by event date and customer hash distribution, set watermarking at 5-10 minutes for latency/completeness balance, enable Photon acceleration for cost efficiency, and implement Purview lineage through Delta's automatic metadata capture.

questions.question: Design a privacy-preserving Azure data platform workflow for a multi-tenant analytics SaaS where each tenant's PII is encrypted at rest, access is isolated by per-tenant RBAC, and end-to-end data lineage is captured across ADLS Gen2, Azure Synapse, and Purview. Include key management strategy (CMKs in Key Vault), per-tenant field-level encryption, and validation of security and lineage in production?

Tenant-scoped CMKs in Azure Key Vault protect ADLS Gen2 and Synapse data at rest; implement envelope encryption for PII in Spark/Databricks transforms before writes; enforce per-tenant RBAC via AAD gr

Use Event Hubs → Databricks Structured Streaming → Delta Lake on ADLS Gen2 with schema evolution enabled. Upsert into a Delta table on (tenant_id, event_id) using MERGE; apply a 5-minute watermark for

questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked

Tests ability to design a compliant, scalable data platform that respects tenant isolation and privacy, while maintaining observable lineage across Azure services.

## Key Concepts

- Tenant isolation and RBAC
- Customer-managed keys and envelope encryption
- Field-level encryption strategies in Spark/Databricks
- End-to-end lineage with Purview
- Production-grade validation and monitoring

## Code Example

```javascript
// Pseudo code: map tenant to CMK and perform envelope encryption on PII before write
function encryptPII(tenantId, plaintext) {
  const cmk = keyVault.getKeyForTenant(tenantId);
  return crypto.aesGcmSeal(plaintext, cmk);
}
```

## Follow-up Questions

- How would you rotate CMKs with zero-downtime for tenants?
- How would you verify lineage completeness when new tenants enroll?

questions.explanation:
This interview question evaluates a candidate's practical understanding of building end-to-end Azure data pipelines with modern best practices. It covers critical concepts including multi-source ingestion orchestration, adaptive schema handling for evolving data sources, pre-load data quality validation with version control, and comprehensive data governance through Purview lineage tracking.

The two-stage pipeline approach demonstrates fundamental Data Factory design patterns: staging raw data for auditability and reprocessing, followed by transformation and loading into a curated star schema. Schema drift handling is essential in production environments where source systems evolve, making drift-tolerant transforms a key skill. The addition of a reusable Python validation activity introduces custom logic beyond Data Factory's built-in capabilities, testing the candidate's ability to integrate external compute for data quality gates.

The versioned schema component evaluates understanding of schema governance, including how to store, version, and roll back schemas when breaking changes occur. Discussion of Purview lineage assesses knowledge of data governance tools and the ability to implement end-to-end traceability from source to destination.

questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked
Tests practical end-to-end ingestion with multiple sources, idempotent upserts, late-data handling, and governance hooks using beginner-friendly Azure services.

## Key Concepts
- Ingest from SFTP to ADLS Gen2 staging
- Delta Lake upserts into Synapse via composite key
- Watermark for late data handling
- Deduplication and failed-store routing
- Lightweight Databricks validation
- Purview lineage integration

## Code Example
```javascript
// Pseudo-logic for upsert into Delta table
const staging = loadDelta('/incoming/partner*/staging')
const target = loadDelta('/synapse/delta/table')
staging.merge(target, ['partner','record_id'])
```

## Follow-up Questions
- How would you validate idempotency and test late-arrival handling?
- How would you extend to add support for a new partner with a different schema without code changes?

questions.explanation:
## Why This Question Is Asked

This question assesses the ability to design a scalable, governable multi-region data lakehouse with end-to-end lineage, real-world data drift handling, and operational resilience. It evaluates understanding of cross-region data movement patterns, SCD Type 2 implementation in a central star schema, data masking in the BI layer, and comprehensive failover and backfill strategies.

## Key Concepts

- **Cross-region ADLS Gen2 replication:** Geo-redundant storage versus active replication; cost implications and consistency trade-offs
- **Delta Lake with MERGE upserts:** Implementing SCD Type 2 with effective start/end dates; handling late-arriving data through idempotent operations
- **Medallion architecture:** Bronze (raw), silver (cleansed), gold (business-ready) layers enabling progressive data quality improvement
- **Synapse star schema:** Central fact tables with conformed dimension tables supporting analytical workloads
- **Purview governance:** Automated lineage tracking across ADF, Databricks, and Synapse; sensitive data classification and masking policies

## What Distinguishes a Strong Answer

A strong response demonstrates practical understanding of consistency models across regions, shows how late-arriving data propagates through the medallion layers, and articulates a concrete backfill strategy using idempotent operations. The candidate should also address operational considerations such as monitoring, alerting, and cost management at scale.

questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked
Assess ability to design a scalable, governance-aware data mesh that handles multi-source ingestion, schema drift, and lineage in a large org.

## Key Concepts
- Data mesh with domain-owned data products
- Contract-driven schema registry and drift handling
- Delta Lake on ADLS Gen2 with versioned schemas
- End-to-end lineage in Purview
- Data quality gates (Great Expectations) and validation notebooks
- Cross-region backfills and replication for resiliency

## Code Example
```javascript
// Example: simple schema check against registry
function validateRecord(schema, record) {
  // pseudo validator
  return Object.keys(schema).every(k => k in record);
}
```

## Follow-up Questions
- How would you version contracts and migrate consumers?
- How would you handle late-arriving data for backfills?

questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked

Tests multi-tenant governance, isolation, and end-to-end lineage in a practical Azure data platform. Assesses ability to combine storage security, ingestion orchestration, drift handling, and data masking with automated validation.

## Key Concepts

- Multi-tenant isolation with per-tenant folders and ACLs
- End-to-end lineage through Purview across ADLS Gen2, Data Factory, and Synapse
- Drift-tolerant incremental loads into Delta tables in Synapse
- Read-time PII masking and synthetic data tests for contract validation
- Cost, SLA alignment, and governance considerations

## Code Example

```python
# PySpark snippet: upsert into Delta table with schema drift tolerance
from delta.tables import DeltaTable
new_df = spark.read.json("abfss://...")
delta_tbl = DeltaTable.forPath(spark, "/mnt/datalake/tenantA/transactions")
delta_tbl.alias("t").merge(
  new_df.alias("s"),
  "t.id = s.id"
).whenMatchedUpdateAll().whenNotMatchedInsertAll().execute()
```

## Follow-up Questions

- How would you test schema drift under high throughput and roll back if contracts change?
- How would you handle cross-tenant data masking policy changes without downtime?

## Key Concepts
- Delta Lake on ADLS Gen2 with schema drift tolerance
- Delta Live Tables for unified ingest
- Row-Level Security in Synapse
- Purview lineage and data quality gates
- Cross-region failover and 7-day backfill
- Cost-conscious partitioning and autoscale

## Code Example
```sql
MERGE INTO [dbo].[DimTenant] AS target
USING staging.Tenant AS src
ON target.tenant_id = src.tenant_id
WHEN MATCHED THEN UPDATE SET ...
WHEN NOT MATCHED THEN INSERT (...)
```

## Follow-up Questions
- How would you validate backfill performance at scale?
- How would you propagate tenant metadata changes to Purview without downtime?

questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked
Tests ability to design multi-tenant data lakes with schema drift, governance, and cost controls in Azure.

## Key Concepts
- Multi-tenancy and per-tenant isolation
- Per-tenant schema inference and drift tolerance
- Delta Lake partitioning and late data handling
- Synapse Row-Level Security and Purview lineage
- SFTP + Event Hubs ingestion and Spark processing

## Code Example
```sql
MERGE INTO curated.transactions AS t
USING staging.transactions AS s
ON t.tenant_id = s.tenant_id AND t.id = s.id
WHEN MATCHED THEN UPDATE SET t.amount = s.amount, t.updated_at = SYSUTCDATETIME()
WHEN NOT MATCHED THEN INSERT (tenant_id, id, amount, updated_at) VALUES (s.tenant_id, s.id, s.amount, SYSUTCDATETIME());
```

```sql
CREATE SECURITY POLICY TenantFilter
ADD FILTER PREDICATE (tenant_id = SYSTEM_USER) ON curated.transactions
WITH (STATE = on);
```

## Follow-up Questions
- How would you monitor lineage changes when a new tenant adds fields?
- How would you tune partitioning for cost and performance across tenants?

Use Synapse Pipelines/ADF to orchestrate ingestion; Spark for schema inference and drift handling with late-arrival retries; store raw JSONL in ADLS Gen2, then create a Star schema (FACT/DIM) in Synap

questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked
Tests end-to-end orchestration across Azure and Snowflake, handling schema drift, late data, and lineage. It checks practical decisions on tooling, data modeling, and resiliency.

## Key Concepts
- Azure Synapse Pipelines / Data Factory orchestration
- Spark-based schema inference and drift handling
- Star schema modeling in Synapse (FACT/DIM) with incremental loads
- Snowflake Snowpipe and external stage integration from ADLS Gen2
- Purview lineage and data governance
- Cross-region DR and data partitioning by date

## Code Example
```text
Pseudo steps:
1. Ingest JSONL to landing area; run schema inference via Spark; store inferred schema metadata.
2. Transform and upsert to STAR in Synapse; maintain watermark for incremental loads.
3. Create Snowflake external stage to ADLS Gen2; enable Snowpipe to load new files; implement idempotent upserts.
4. Register datasets in Purview for end-to-end lineage.
5. Configure geo-redundant storage and cross-region replication for DR.
```

## Follow-up Questions
- How would you handle nested JSON and evolving field paths? 
- How would you validate cross-region data freshness and retry policies?

questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked
Demonstrates practical use of Azure Data Factory with simple drift handling, idempotent loading, and governance.

## Key Concepts
- Data Factory pipelines
- Mapping Data Flows
- Parquet storage and raw/curated separation
- MERGE-based upserts with watermark
- Purview lineage

## Code Example
```sql
MERGE INTO dbo.FactSales AS t
USING staging.FactSales AS s
ON t.BusinessKey = s.BusinessKey
WHEN MATCHED THEN UPDATE SET t.Amount = s.Amount
WHEN NOT MATCHED THEN INSERT (BusinessKey, Amount) VALUES (s.BusinessKey, s.Amount);
```

## Follow-up Questions
- How would you test idempotency and drift handling?
- How to extend to more sources without breaking schema?

questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked
Tests ability to design a scalable, compliant, and observable data platform that handles multi-tenant isolation, schema drift, and cross-region resilience in a real-world banking context.

## Key Concepts
- Multi-tenant isolation and prefix-based governance
- Delta Lake upserts (MERGE) and late-arriving data handling
- Schema drift detection and evolution policies
- Purview lineage and tenant-scoped data sources
- Region residency with landing zones and geo-redundancy
- Monitoring, rollback via Delta time travel, and failover strategies

## Code Example
```sql
MERGE INTO delta.`/mnt/adls/tenantA/table` AS t
USING updates AS s
ON t.id = s.id
WHEN MATCHED THEN UPDATE SET t.value = s.value
WHEN NOT MATCHED THEN INSERT (id, value) VALUES (s.id, s.value);
```

## Follow-up Questions
- How would you validate cross-region failover with minimal risk?
- What tests would you add to ensure Purview lineage accuracy over time?

questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked
Tests end-to-end Azure data engineering skills: REST ingestion, storage format choices, and simple Spark-based transformations, plus incremental loads and lineage.

## Key Concepts
- Data Factory REST ingestion
- Parquet in ADLS Gen2
- Databricks for flattening nested JSON
- Azure Synapse star schema
- Watermark-based incremental loads
- Purview lineage and basic data quality checks

## Code Example
```javascript
// Example: simple watermark check (pseudo)
function shouldLoad(record, watermark) {
  return new Date(record.ingest_ts) > new Date(watermark);
}
```

## Follow-up Questions
- How would you monitor pipeline health and alert on failures?
- How would you adapt this if REST API rate limits change?

questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked

Explores practical design of schema evolution, data contracts, and lineage in Azure for multi-tenant data products.

## Key Concepts

- Delta Lake schema evolution and time travel
- Data contracts and schema registry in Purview/SQL
- End-to-end lineage across ADF/Databricks to Purview
- Multi-tenant isolation and rollback semantics

## Code Example

```scala
// Spark drift check placeholder
val current = df.schema
val contract = readContract("productA")
assert(current == contract.schema, "Schema drift detected")
```

## Follow-up Questions

- How would you test the drift detection and rollbacks in CI/CD?
- How do you alert/monitor schema drift and contract violations?

## Why This Is Asked
This question probes end-to-end Azure data engineering capabilities across streaming and batch, governance, and cost-aware resilience.

## Key Concepts
- Delta Lake on ADLS Gen2 with schema evolution
- Delta Live Tables or Spark streaming with MERGE upserts
- Synapse star schema and serverless SQL
- Purview lineage and data governance
- Per-tenant row-level security and access control
- Cross-region backups and backfill strategies

## Code Example
```sql
MERGE INTO star_schema.fact AS f
USING updates AS u
ON f.key = u.key
WHEN MATCHED THEN UPDATE SET /* fields */
WHEN NOT MATCHED THEN INSERT /* fields */;
```

## Follow-up Questions
- How would you validate lineage accuracy end-to-end?
- How do you handle late-arriving data and schema drift in production?

questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked
Tests ability to design streaming pipeline across Azure components with real-time data, schema drift, CDC-like behavior, and governance.

## Key Concepts
- Databricks Auto Loader, Delta Lake, mergeSchema
- Bronze-Silver-Gold streaming path
- Azure Purview for end-to-end lineage
- Azure Synapse Analytics star schema serving
- Late-arriving data handling, watermarking, checkpointing, rollback

## Code Example
```javascript
// Bronze: streaming read (Auto Loader-like)
const bronze = spark.readStream
  .format('cloudFiles')
  .option('cloudFiles.format','json')
  .option('cloudFiles.inferColumnTypes','true')
  .load('abfss://container@storage.dfs.core.windows.net/bronze');

// Silver: upsert into Delta with schema drift tolerance
bronze.writeStream
  .format('delta')
  .option('checkpointLocation','/mnt/checkpoints/silver')
  .start('/mnt/delta/silver');

DeltaTable.forPath(spark, '/mnt/delta/silver').as('s').merge(
  bronze.alias('b'), 's.id = b.id'
).whenMatchedUpdateAll().whenNotMatchedInsertAll().execute();
```

## Follow-up Questions
- How would you verify end-to-end lineage across Purview and Synapse?
- How would you validate schema drift scenarios and rollback correctness?

## Why This Is Asked

This question tests practical design of real-time CDC pipelines across on-prem to Azure, handling drift, late data, upserts, and governance.

## Key Concepts

- CDC ingestion, Spark Structured Streaming, Delta Lake schema evolution, MERGE upserts, Purview lineage, watermarking, late data handling, idempotent writes, monitoring.

## Code Example

```sql
MERGE INTO delta.`/datalake/sa/star/fact_sales` AS target
USING updates AS source
ON target.sale_id = source.sale_id
WHEN MATCHED THEN UPDATE SET target.* = source.*
WHEN NOT MATCHED THEN INSERT *;
```

## Follow-up Questions

- How would you validate lineage end-to-end across sources and sinks in Purview?
- How would you implement backfill for late data without duplicate counts?

questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked

Tests practical ability to handle cross-partner schema drift with minimal code, while ensuring incremental loads, data quality, and lineage in a beginner context.

## Key Concepts

- Azure Data Factory pipelines and Data Flows for per-partner mapping
- Landing to ADLS Gen2 and Parquet as the intermediate format
- Watermark-based incremental loads into Synapse star schema
- Purview lineage integration via the Data Factory integration runtime
- Handling SFTP ingestion and date partitioning

## Code Example

```sql
MERGE INTO dbo.FactSales AS t
USING staging.FactSales AS s
ON t.SaleKey = s.SaleKey
WHEN MATCHED THEN UPDATE SET t.Amount = s.Amount, t.DateKey = s.DateKey
WHEN NOT MATCHED THEN INSERT (SaleKey, Amount, DateKey) VALUES (s.SaleKey, s.Amount, s.DateKey);
```

## Follow-up Questions

- How would you test the per-partner mappings to guard against drift?
- How would you monitor for late data and ensure idempotent upserts?
